title: Emergency
lead: Inspired mainly by the ==Weimar Constitution==, the US and Australia. ==‘In times of crisis, the Constitution dons its armour — but does it risk losing its soul?’== — Justice H. R. Khanna.

sections.title: The Three Emergencies at a Glance

items.label: President's Rule impositions — first in Punjab (1951); frequent in the 1970s–90s

items.title: Grounds, Approval, Duration
bullets: Grounds: ==external aggression or armed rebellion== (‘internal disturbance’ replaced by ==44th CA, 1978==)
==Union Cabinet must recommend in writing== (44th CA); proclamation valid 6 months
Approval: LS & RS with ==Special Majority II within 30 days==; extension ==indefinitely, 6 months at a time==
Revocation: by President; or ==LS simple majority== — 1/10th of members can requisition a special sitting ==within 14 days==
Can be imposed ==before, during or after== aggression/rebellion

items.title: Impact
bullets: Union executive can ==direct any State== in the emergency area; Parliament legislates on the ==State List==
President can ==alter revenue distribution== to states (orders placed before Parliament)
LS and SLAs extendable ==1 year at a time== (max 6 months after emergency ends)
==Art 19 automatically suspended== only for external aggression (44th CA); ==Arts 20 & 21 can never be suspended== (44th CA)
Makes India ==unitary== — but state legislatures/executives are NOT dissolved

items.title: Issues & Cases
bullets: ==Concentration of power==; suspension of the federal structure; extension of legislative tenures
[[ADM Jabalpur (1976)]] (Habeas Corpus case): life & liberty suspendable — ==effectively overturned by the 44th CA==
[[Minerva Mills (1980)]]: proclamation is ==subject to judicial review== (on constitutional limits, not adequacy of reasons)

items.title: Global Practice & Reform
bullets: Germany/US: ==judicial or legislative oversight== of declarations; time-bound proclamations with periodic approval
Germany's Basic Law protects ==core rights even in emergency==
Reforms: parliamentary review, robust judicial review, ==clear objective criteria==, transparency and public debate

items.title: Grounds, Approval, Duration
bullets: Ground: ==failure of the state's constitutional machinery== (read with Art 355 duty of the Union)
Governor may recommend — but ==can be imposed without it==
Approval: both Houses, ==simple majority within 60 days==; extension ==up to 3 years==, 6 months at a time
Beyond 1 year only if ==National Emergency is in force== or ==ECI certifies== elections can't be held
==SLA can't be dissolved before Parliament approves== ([[SR Bommai (1994)]])

items.title: Impact
bullets: President may assume ==state executive functions== and Governor's powers
State legislature's powers exercised ==by or under Parliament's authority==
==HC powers cannot be subsumed==

head: Proper grounds
Improper grounds
rows: Acting ==contrary to the Constitution== or Union directives
Failure to meet an extraordinary situation; security concerns
Power used ‘merely for achieving good government’; mere ==‘internal disturbance’==

head: Case
Holding
rows: President's satisfaction reviewable if ==mala fide or on irrelevant grounds==.
Art 356 used ==sparingly==; ==floor test== determines majority; proclamations judicially reviewable; no dissolution before parliamentary approval; courts can ==restore dismissed governments==.
Dissolving a fresh assembly on ==anticipated horse-trading== is unconstitutional.

blocks.text: ==Sarkaria's way forward==: exhaust all state-level alternatives first; Governor's report as a ==‘speaking document’==; strict parliamentary review; robust judicial oversight; vigilant media and civil society.

items: Ground: threat to ==India's financial stability or credit==; President must be satisfied.
Approval: both Houses, ==simple majority within 60 days==; duration ==indefinite==; revoked by President.
Impact: Union can direct states on ==financial propriety== — including ==reducing salaries== of state employees; President can similarly cut Union employees' and ==SC/HC judges'== salaries.

blocks.text: Emergency provisions test the resilience of a democracy — not by how swiftly power is centralised in crisis, but by how quickly it is relinquished once the crisis is over. India's constitutional architects envisioned emergency powers as a necessary evil — to protect the nation, not to dominate its people.
